behaviors.description: Content passed to summary-generation includes data from all three sources
behaviors.justification: A pure Unit test (mocking all three sources) could pass even if the code only calls two of them or fails to merge data. The real failure is: code skips one source or fails to wire data correctly. At Integration level, mock the HTTP client layer but verify that the data-collection module actually invokes all three sources and passes their complete data downstream. This catches bugs in inter-module wiring that mocks at the function boundary would hide.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the HTTP client layer for all three data sources to return test data. Verify that the data-collection module calls all three sources and that the summary-generation step receives a composite input containing data from all three. Assert that any missing source is detected as a test failure.

behaviors.description: One unavailable source produces 'data unavailable' note instead of aborting
behaviors.justification: Unit testing with mocked exceptions could work if you mock all possible error types (ConnectionError, Timeout, HTTPError variants, etc.), but real APIs often raise error types not anticipated in tests. The real failure is: error handling is incomplete or the 'unavailable' note is not actually included in output. Integration level tests the error-handling logic in context of the data-fetching module, using realistic error types at the HTTP client boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Mock one data source HTTP client to raise a realistic error (e.g., asyncio.TimeoutError or http.HTTPError). Verify that the report-generation pipeline catches the error, continues processing, and includes a 'data unavailable' note for that section in the final report. Repeat with multiple error types (connection, timeout, server error).

behaviors.description: Narrative summary accurately represents the key values in the input data
behaviors.justification: The word 'narrative' implies natural-language generation, likely via LLM. A Unit or Integration test that mocks the LLM would always return expected values, hiding real failures: dropped values, hallucinations, or misrepresented numbers. The real failure is: the LLM-generated summary is inaccurate despite correct input. Only a real LLM call at Agentic level can catch whether the model actually captures and represents the data correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM service with test data containing specific, measurable values (e.g., 'deployment count: 5', 'P1 incidents: 3', 'uptime: 99.8%'). Generate the narrative summary and verify that the output includes these values with correct magnitude and meaning. Use multiple test cases to verify accuracy is maintained across different value ranges and data combinations.

behaviors.description: Complete report from data collection through email delivery reaches recipient's inbox correctly
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end behavior spanning multiple real components: data collection (requires real or test API endpoints), LLM summary generation (Agentic—requires real LLM call), and email delivery (requires real SMTP or email service). The real failure is a break anywhere in the pipeline: data not fetched, summary generation fails, email not delivered, or content malformed. No lower level can catch this because you must verify all steps execute correctly and data flows correctly through the entire chain.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full script end-to-end using test endpoints for the three data sources and a test email account. Verify that an email arrives at the configured recipient with: (1) data from all three sources present, (2) a narrative summary that accurately represents key values, (3) correct report structure and formatting. This tests the entire workflow including LLM invocation and email delivery integration.

behaviors.description: When email delivery is unreachable, report is saved locally and retried on next scheduled run
behaviors.justification: This behavior has two parts: (1) save report to file when email fails (testable at Integration), and (2) retry on next scheduled run (requires System level). The real failure is: file is written to the wrong location, scheduler doesn't execute the retry, or the retry logic doesn't correctly read and resend. Testing the file write alone (Integration) won't catch scheduler-level bugs. System level is needed to verify the scheduled job re-runs and correctly handles the retry workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the email service to fail with a connection error. Verify that the script saves the generated report to the configured local path and does not crash. Then manually trigger the script a second time (simulating the next scheduled run) and verify that it reads the saved file, attempts to resend, and deletes the file upon success. Test also that the file persists if retry fails, and is eventually cleaned up or marked for manual review.
